heading:
CONSTRUCTIVE EVICTION  
(CAL. CIV. CODE §§ 789.3 and 1940.02 et seq.)
(Plaintiff Against All Defendants and DOES 1-25)
insertText:
{n}	PLAINTIFF realleges and incorporates by reference every allegation contained in paragraphs 1 through 62 of this Complaint as though fully set forth in this paragraph.
{n}	PLAINTIFF held a leasehold interest in and had been a tenant of the property while the DEFENDANTS named in this cause of action have owned and/or managed the property.
{n}	As landowners and/or managers of a land or estate used as a residential dwelling, the DEFENDANTS, and each of them, owed a duty of care under common law and California Civil Code section 789.3 to refrain from directly or indirectly causing the interruption or termination of any utility service, whether or not the landlord controlled the utility service.
{n}	As landowners and/or managers of a land or estate used as a residential dwelling, the DEFENDANTS, and each of them, owed a duty of care under California Civil Code section 1940.2, which prohibits landlords from using or threatening to use force, intimidation, or menacing behavior to force lawful tenants to leave their dwelling.
{n}	It is well established in California and under common law that a landlord commits a constructive eviction when the landlord, or someone acting under the landlord’s authority directly or indirectly causes any disturbance of a tenant's quiet possession, whereby the premises are rendered unfit for occupancy for the purpose for which they are designed, or if the tenant is deprived of the beneficial enjoyment of the premises.
{n}	At all relevant times herein, DEFENDANTS’ duties as alleged herein extended to PLAINTIFF.
{n}	DEFENDANTS breached their common law and statutory duties by causing the interruption of services to the PLAINTIFF’s home, depriving the PLAINTIFF of the quiet enjoyment of {pronoun_possessive} home, and rendering the premises untenantable as a residential dwelling.  DEFENDANTS knew, or reasonably should have known, that PLAINTIFF would be injured as a result of their breach of the common law and statutory duties.
{n}	DEFENDANTS knew or should have known that PLAINTIFF would suffer foreseeable injuries, damages, and harm as a result of DEFENDANTS’ failure to exercise reasonable ordinary care as alleged above.
{n}	As a direct and proximate result of the DEFENDANTS’ continuous conduct, the value of the PLAINTIFF’S leasehold was diminished. Consequently, PLAINTIFF has been damaged and injured in an amount to be proven at trial.
{n}	Furthermore, as a direct and proximate result of the DEFENDANTS’ conduct, specifically PLAINTIFF was damaged and injured by having to surrender {pronoun_possessive} leasehold and incur significant moving expenses. In addition, PLAINTIFF was also damaged by having to hire licensed experts to visit and/or inspect the PROPERTY due to the DEFENDANTS’ negligence and lack of candor or care towards PLAINTIFF and {pronoun_possessive} tenancy.
{n}	Further still, PLAINTIFF was damaged and injured as a direct and proximate result of the DEFENDANTS’ conduct, and pursuant to California Civil Code section 789.3, subdivision (c)(2), PLAINTIFF is entitled to statutory damages and reasonable attorneys’ fees.

checkboxText: Breach of the Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment
heading:
BREACH OF THE COVENANT OF QUIET ENJOYMENT
(Plaintiff Against All Defendants and DOES 1-25)
insertText:
{n}PLAINTIFF realleges and incorporates by reference every allegation contained in this Complaint as though fully set forth in this paragraph.
{n}PLAINTIFF has held a leasehold interest in and has been tenant of the PROPERTY, while the DEFENDANTS named in this cause of action have owned and/or managed the property.
{n}Implied in each rental agreement in California, oral or written, is a covenant that the landlord will not interfere with the tenant’s quiet enjoyment of the premises during the term of {pronoun_possessive} tenancy. California Civil Code section 1927 specifically provides that “(a)n agreement to let upon hire binds the letter to secure the hirer the quiet possession of the thing hired during the term of the hiring…,” and as such, this duty has applied to DEFENDANTS as owners and/or managers of the property.
{n}DEFENDANTS have and always have had a duty to abide by the implied covenant of quiet enjoyment. DEFENDANTS breached this duty and the implied covenant by their conduct described above, including, but not limited to, (1) abusing the right of entry to the premises; (2) severe tenant harassment by DEFENDANTS’ agents; (3) demanding or coercing PLAINTIFF to tender, and ultimately collecting rents in excess of those permissible under law or contract; (4) verbal eviction threats against PLAINTIFF; (5) abusing civil process and maliciously attempting to evict the tenant without just cause; (6) failing to repair in reasonable time and fashion; (7) failing to repair unsafe, unsanitary, and uninhabitable conditions at the PROPERTY; and (8) failing to maintain the PROPERTY in a habitable condition.
{n}DEFENDANTS knew, reasonably should have known, or would have learned through reasonable inspection, that PLAINTIFF would suffer damage as a result of DEFENDANTS’ obvious breach. PLAINTIFF and various entities sent DEFENDANTS and their agents numerous complaints regarding the uninhabitable conditions, and as such, DEFENDANTS and their agents were made aware on multiple occasions of the uninhabitable and untenantable conditions at the PROPERTY, which breached PLAINTIFF’s right to the quiet enjoyment of {pronoun_possessive} home. PLAINTIFF frequently advised DEFENDANTS to correct measures affecting {pronoun_possessive} ability to use the PROPERTY without fear or threat, and that the amount of rent that they were attempting to collect was improperly high without corrective measures. Furthermore, DEFENDANTS knew, or should have known, that they were not entitled to enter PLAINTIFF’s home absent exigent circumstances or upon proper notice and for a proper purpose. Finally, DEFENDANTS knew, or should have known, that they should not have harassed PLAINTIFF, due to the litany of habitability concerns PLAINTIFF faced, nor should they institute any notice to vacate without any adequate or proper cause.
{n}California Civil Code section 1940.2 provides in pertinent part that: “(a) It is unlawful for a landlord to do any of the following for the purpose of influencing a tenant to vacate a dwelling… [:] (3) Use, or threaten to use, force, willful threats, or menacing conduct constituting a course of conduct that interferes with the tenant's quiet enjoyment of the premises in violation of Section 1927 that would create an apprehension of harm in a reasonable person. Nothing in this paragraph requires a tenant to be actually or constructively evicted in order to obtain relief. . . . (b) A tenant who prevails in a civil action, including an action in small claims court, to enforce {pronoun_possessive} rights under this section is entitled to a civil penalty in an amount not to exceed two thousand dollars ($2,000) for each violation.”
{n}DEFENDANTS, and each of them, have engaged in a systematic and ongoing pattern of willful threats and menacing conduct towards PLAINTIFF, interfering with {pronoun_possessive} quiet enjoyment of {pronoun_possessive} home as outlined throughout this Complaint.
{n}As a direct and proximate result of DEFENDANTS’ breaches of the covenant of quiet enjoyment, the value of PLAINTIFF’s leasehold was and has been diminished. Consequently, PLAINTIFF was and has been damaged in an amount equal to the rental payments due and paid during PLAINTIFF’S leasehold, or in an amount to be proven at trial.
{n}As a direct and proximate result of DEFENDANTS’ conduct, PLAINTIFF has suffered illness, physical injury, mental stress, emotional distress, discomfort, annoyance, depression, anxiety, fear for safety, loss in the leasehold’s value, property damage, and lost income, all to PLAINTIFF’s damage in an amount to be determined according to proof at trial, but which amount is within this Court’s jurisdictional requirements.
{n}DEFENDANTS have, individually and in concert, acted with recklessness and conscious disregard of PLAINTIFF’s rights. DEFENDANTS’ conduct in breaching the covenant of quiet enjoyment has been grossly negligent, malicious, and oppressive, and thereby entitles PLAINTIFF to punitive damages in an amount to be determined at trial.

{n}	California Civil Code section 1940.2 provides in pertinent part that: “(a) It is unlawful for a landlord to do any of the following for the purpose of influencing a tenant to vacate a dwelling… [:] (3) Use, or threaten to use, force, willful threats, or menacing conduct constituting a course of conduct that interferes with the tenant’s quiet enjoyment of the premises in violation of [s]ection 1927 that would create an apprehension of harm in a reasonable person. Nothing in this paragraph requires a tenant to be actually or constructively evicted in order to obtain relief. . . . (b) A tenant who prevails in a civil action, including an action in small claims court, to enforce {pronoun_possessive} rights under this section is entitled to a civil penalty in an amount not to exceed two thousand dollars ($2,000.00) for each violation.” 
{n}	DEFENDANTS’ consistent failure to make the PROPERTY reasonably safe for PLAINTIFF, as well as their continuous failures to make necessary repairs, constitutes a course of conduct that has interfered with PLAINTIFF’s quiet enjoyment of the PROPERTY and would create an apprehension of harm in a reasonable person.
{n}	DEFENDANTS’ actions, including, but not limited to, refusing to make repairs, harassing PLAINTIFF, never hiring proper and or licensed individuals to correct PLAINTIFF’S PROPERTY issues, pressuring and intimidating {pronoun_object} to vacate the PROPERTY, and issuing a frivolous, baseless, and retaliatory notice constitutes a course of conduct that interferes with PLAINTIFF’s quiet enjoyment of the PROPERTY and would create apprehension of harm in a reasonable person. 
{n}	A tenant who prevails in a claim under California Civil Code section 1940.2 is entitled to a civil penalty of up to $2,000.00 for each violation. (§ 1940.2, subd. (b).) PLAINTIFF seeks civil penalties, compensatory damages, special damages, attorneys’ fees, and costs in an amount to be proven at trial.

checkboxText: Violation of the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance
heading:
VIOLATION OF THE LOS ANGELES RENT STABILIZATION ORDINANCE
(Plaintiff Against All Defendants and DOES 1-25)
insertText:
{n}	PLAINTIFF realleges and incorporates by reference every allegation contained in this Complaint as though fully set forth in this paragraph. 
{n}	PLAINTIFF has held a leasehold interest in and has been a tenant of the property while the DEFENDANTS named in this cause of action have owned and/or managed the property. 
{n}	The PROPERTY is a QUAD-PLEX located within the City of Los Angeles, and as such, the rents that may be charged for the units there are controlled and may only be raised pursuant to the authority contained in Los Angeles Municipal Code section 151.00, et seq.  The Los Angeles Housing Department, through its Rent Adjustment Commission, promulgates regulations that control the rents that may be charged by landlords or housing services.
{n}	DEFENDANTS named herein are “landlords” as defined by Los Angeles Municipal Code (“L.A.M.C.”) section 151.02.
{n}	The provision of natural water is a “housing service” as defined by the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance at L.A.M.C. section 151.02. 
{n}	The provision of heat is a “housing service” as defined by the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance at L.A.M.C. section 151.02.  
{n}	The provision of ordinary repairs is a “housing service” as defined by the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance at L.A.M.C. section 151.02.  
{n}	The provision of maintenance is a “housing service” as defined by the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance at L.A.M.C. section 151.02.  
{n}	The provision of painting is a “housing service” as defined by the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance at L.A.M.C. section 151.02.  
{n}	The provision of refuse removal is a “housing service” as defined by the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance at L.A.M.C. section 151.02.  
{n}	DEFENDANTS’ failure to provide, inter alia, proper maintenance, heat, and gas constitutes reductions of housing services, and therefore constitutes “rent increases” as defined by Los Angeles Municipal Code section 151.02. The reduction of housing services without a corresponding reduction of rent results in a rental increase above that amount approved by the Rent Adjustment Commissions, which promulgates regulations under the authority of L.A.M.C. section 151.03. The value of the reduction in housing services is no less than $500.00 per month per unit.
{n}	DEFENDANTS have, by reducing housing services, illegally raised PLAINTIFF’s rent in violation of the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance. PLAINTIFF alleges that the value of the reduced housing services is $500.00 per month per unit, and that therefore DEFENDANTS have raised their rents per year without obtaining permission from the Los Angeles Housing Department.
{n}	DEFENDANTS have been wrongly charging, accepting, and retaining monies in the form of illegal rent increases that belong to PLAINTIFF.
{n}	Pursuant to L.A.M.C. section 151.10A, PLAINTIFF is entitled to damages equal to those funds DEFENDANTS illegally collected and retained and are entitled to treble damages thereon.  
{n}	PLAINTIFF is further entitled to recover reasonable attorney’s fees and costs in connection with this action.

heading:

[VIOLATION OF CIVIL CODE § 1950.5]

If deductions for cleaning or repairs exceeded $125, DEFENDANTS were required to provide copies of receipts, invoices, or a good faith estimate.

heading:
HARASSMENT IN VIOLATION OF L.A.M.C. SECTION §45.33
(Plaintiff Against All Defendants and DOES 1-25)
insertText:
{n}	Plaintiff repeats and incorporates the allegations set forth in paragraphs 1 to 68. 
{n}	As defined by L.A.M.C. section 45.32, Defendants are a “Landlord”; the 
PROPERTY is a “Rental Unit”; and Plaintiff is a “Tenant.”
{n}	Tenant harassment is defined by L.A.M.C. as “a landlord’s bad faith conduct  
directed at a specific tenant or tenants that causes the latter detriment or harm. “Bad faith” refers to willful, reckless, or grossly negligent conduct. Examples of Tenant Harassment include[,] but are not limited to[,] the following actions: 
1.	 Reducing or eliminating, or threatening to reduce or eliminate, housing 
services required by a lease, contract [,] or  law, including the elimination of parking if provided in the tenant’s lease or contract except when necessary to comply with a court order or local or state law, or to create an accessory dwelling unit or additional housing.
2.	Failing to perform and timely complete necessary repairs and maintenance required by Federal, State, County, or local housing, health, or safety laws; or failure to follow applicable industry standards to minimize exposure to noise, dust, lead paint, asbestos, or other building materials with potentially harmful health impacts.
3.	Abuse of the right of access into a rental unit as established and limited by California Civil Code [s]ection 1954, including the following:  entering or photographing portions of a rental unit that are beyond the scope of a lawful entry or inspection. . . .
4.	Threatening a tenant, or their guests, by word or gesture, with physical harm.
5.	Attempting to coerce the tenant to vacate with offer(s) of payments.
6.	Representing to a tenant that the tenant is required to vacate a rental unit or enticing a tenant to vacate a rental unit through a misrepresentation or the concealment or omission of a material fact.
7.	Threatening or taking action to terminate any tenancy[,] including service of any notice to quit or other eviction notice or bringing action to recover possession of a rental unit based on facts [that] the landlord has no reasonable cause to believe to be true. No landlord shall be liable under this subsection for bringing an action to recover possession of a rental unit unless and until the tenant has obtained a favorable termination of that action.
8.	Threatening or engaging in any act or omission [that] interferes with the tenant’s right to use and enjoy the rental unit or whereby the premises are rendered unfit for human habitation and occupancy.
9.	Refusing to acknowledge, facilitate, or accept receipt of lawful rent payments or rental assistance payments as set forth in the lease agreement or as established by the usual practice of the parties or applicable law. . . .
10.-11.	[Omitted]
12.	Disclosing or threatening to disclose information about a tenant to any government entity for engaging in legally protected activities or to influence them to vacate.
13.-16. [Omitted]
17.	Other repeated acts or omissions of such significance as to substantially interfere with or disturb the comfort, repose, peace[,] or quiet of a tenant(s) and the cause, are likely to cause, or are committed with the objective to cause a tenant(s) to surrender or waive any rights in relation to such tenancy.
{n}	Because the PROPERTY is subject to the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization 
Ordinance (LARSO), tenant harassment also encompasses “offering payments to a tenant to vacate their unit without providing written notice to the tenant of their rights under [s]ection 151.31 of this code… using the form prescribed by the Department.” (L.A.M.C. § 151.33.)
{n}	DEFENDANTS’ acts, as described herein, constitute “bad faith” conduct directed
at PLAINTIFF (i.e., a specific tenant) that has caused detriment and harm. DEFENDANTS’ conduct constitutes Tenant Harassment within the above definition, as described herein.
{n}	Defendants have always acted with a continuity of purpose, i.e., to unlawfully 
remove PLAINTIFF from the PROPERTY.
{n}	As a direct and proximate result of DEFENDANTS’ knowing and willful course 
of conduct directed at the PLAINTIFF, PLAINTIFF has suffered past and future special damages and past and future general damages in an amount to be determined according to proof, but which amount is within this Court’s jurisdictional requirements. PLAINTIFF has emotional and financial damages, including, but not limited to, suffering anxiety, depression, emotional distress, and diminished quality of living conditions.
{n}	Under L.A.M.C. section 45.33B, a prevailing party Plaintiff may be awarded 
compensatory damages. PLAINTIFF has incurred costs specific to DEFENDANTS’ harassment, as described herein. PLAINTIFF has also been generally damaged in an amount to be proven at trial. 
{n}	Under L.A.M.C. section 45.33B, a prevailing Plaintiff is entitled to an award of 
attorneys’ fees and costs incurred for bringing an action under this section. PLAINTIFF has employed and will continue to employ attorneys for the initiation and prosecution of this action. PLAINTIFF has incurred and will continue to incur attorneys’ fees and costs herein. PLAINTIFF is thus entitled to an award of attorneys’ fees and costs.
{n}	Under L.A.M.C. section 45.33B, the Court may impose civil penalties up to 
$10,000 per violation in circumstances where a Plaintiff has prevailed. The appropriate amount of penalties is to be adjudged by the Court, depending on the violation’s severity. The violations in this case are many and severe; thus, PLAINTIFF respectfully requests that DEFENDANTS be made pay the maximum amount of civil penalties.
{n}	DEFENDANTS’ conduct continues to cause significant and irreparable injury to 
PLAINTIFF, who has no adequate remedy at law for the injuries {pronoun_subject} has already suffered, currently suffers, and will suffer, from DEFENDANTS’ continued harassment. Due to the following, injunctive relief is thus necessary and proper, pursuant to L.A.M.C. section 45.33D.
{n}	In engaging in the conduct alleged above, DEFENDANTS, and all agents, 
servants, employees, and authorized representatives, acted with malice, fraud, and oppression, and/or in conscious disregard of PLAINTIFF’s health, rights, and well-being, and intended to subject PLAINTIFF to unjust hardship, thereby warranting an assessment of punitive damages pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 3294, subdivision (a), in an amount sufficient to punish these DEFENDANTS and deter others from engaging in similar conduct. (See L.A.M.C. § 45.35E [stating that the remedies provided for in the Tenant Anti-Harassment Ordinance are not exclusive and do not preclude a tenant from seeking other remedies, penalties, and punitive damages].)

checkboxText: Discriminatory Eviction in Violation of the Unruh Civil Rights Act
heading:
DISCRIMINATORY EVICTION IN VIOLATION OF THE UNRUH CIVIL RIGHTS ACT
[CAL. CIV. CODE § 51 ET. SEQ.]
(Brought Against All Defendants)
insertText:
{n}PLAINTIFF realleges and incorporates by reference every allegation contained in this Complaint as though fully set forth in this paragraph.
{n}PLAINTIFF holds a leasehold interest in and has been a tenant of the property while the Defendants named in this cause of action have owned and/or managed the property.
{n}PLAINTIFF is a member of not less than three (3) separate protected classes in California. PLAINTIFF is gay, is disabled, and is elderly.
{n}It is well established in California that landlords may not exclude persons from housing based on the tenant’s membership in a protected class. Indeed, the California legislature has sharply circumscribed an apartment owner’s traditional discretion to accept and reject tenants on the basis of the landlord’s own likes and dislikes, and has brought such landlords within the embrace of the broad statutory provisions of the Unruh Act.
{n}Furthermore, PLAINTIFF has, and continues to associate with other persons who are members of protected classes. Much to the dismay of the landlords, PLAINTIFF has had houseguests who are other gay men.
{n}Defendants have, individually and in concert, acted to deny PLAINTIFF and exclude PLAINTIFF from housing accommodations based on the fact that PLAINTIFF is an elderly, disabled, gay man who associates with other gay men.
{n}The substantial and motivating reason for landlord initiating proceedings to evict PLAINTIFF from the home was PLAINTIFF’s sexual orientation, age, and disability. Defendants were further substantially motivated in their unlawful conduct by the fact that PLAINTIFF associates with and has houseguests who are also homosexual men.
{n}PLAINTIFF was harmed by landlord’s conduct, and the landlord’s conduct was a substantial and material factor in causing this harm to PLAINTIFF.
{n}As a direct and proximate result of Defendants’ conduct, PLAINTIFF suffered illness, physical injury, mental stress, emotional distress, discomfort, annoyance, depression, anxiety, fear for safety, and loss in the value of the leasehold, all to PLAINTIFF’s damage in an amount to be determined according to proof at trial, but which amount is within the jurisdictional requirements of this Court.
{n}PLAINTIFF is also entitled to statutory damages in an amount not less than the minimum amount of four thousand dollars ($4,000) against each Defendant, as the Defendants, and each of them have denied, aided in the denial, or otherwise incited the denial of housing accommodations to PLAINTIFF based on sexual orientation, age, and disability.
{n}PLAINTIFF is further entitled to recover reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs associated with prosecuting this action against Defendants for acts of discrimination based on sexual orientation, age, and disability in contravention of the Unruh Civil Rights Act.
{n}Defendants’ conduct is so repugnant to public policy and decency that it shocks the conscience, thus entitling PLAINTIFF to punitive damages against Defendants, and each of them, in an amount up to three (3) times the amount of damages as a penalty against the Defendants and each Defendant.

heading:
RETALIATORY EVICTION 
(Brought Against All Defendants)
insertText:
{n}PLAINTIFF realleges and incorporates by reference every allegation contained in this Complaint as though fully set forth in this paragraph.
{n}PLAINTIFF holds a leasehold interest in and has been a tenant of the property while the Defendants named in this cause of action have owned and/or managed the property.
{n}The anti-retaliation provisions of Cal. Civ. Code § 1942.5 prohibit a landlord from raising a tenant’s rent or causing the tenant to quit the premises voluntarily within 180 days after the tenant exercises rights under the relevant chapter of the Civil Code regarding the hiring of real property.
{n}The 180 days runs from the latest applicable date within Cal. Civ. Code § 1942.5. In the instant case, the 180 days would run from March 29, 2013, the date on which PLAINTIFF complained to DEFENDANT about roaches in the apartment and demanded that Defendants cure the vermin infestation.
{n}Both May 14, 2013, the date on which Defendants purportedly served a 60-day Notice to Quit on PLAINTIFF, and July 18, 2013, the date on which M G filed its action for Unlawful Detainer against PLAINTIFF, are both less than 180 days after March 29, 2013.
{n}Any lessor or agent of a lessor who violates § 1942.5 “shall be liable to the lessee in a civil action for all of the following: (1) the actual damages sustained by the lessee; (2) punitive damages in an amount of not less than one hundred dollars ($100) nor more than two thousand dollars ($2,000) for each retaliatory act where the lessor or agent has been guilty of fraud, oppression, or malice with respect to that act.” PLAINTIFF hereby requests punitive damages in the amount of two thousand dollars for each retaliatory act of the Defendants.
{n}Furthermore, § 1942.5(g) provides that in any action brought for damages for retaliatory eviction, the court shall award reasonable attorney’s fees to the prevailing party if either party requests attorney’s fees upon the initiation of the action. § 1942.5(h) provides that “[t]he remedies provided by this section shall be in addition to any other remedies provided by statutory or decisional law.” PLAINTIFF hereby requests reasonable attorneys’ fees for this suit in an amount according to proof.
{n}As a direct and proximate result of Defendants’ conduct, PLAINTIFF suffered illness, physical injury, mental stress, emotional distress, discomfort, annoyance, depression, anxiety, fear for safety, and loss in the value of the leasehold, all to PLAINTIFF’s damage in an amount to be determined according to proof at trial, but which amount is within the jurisdictional requirements of this Court.

insertText:

heading:
PROMISSORY ESTOPPEL 
(Brought Against All Defendants)

checkboxText: California Welfare and Institutions Code § 15600 et seq.
heading:
California Welfare and Institutions Code§ 15600, et seq.
(By All Plaintiffs Against All Defendants and DOES 1-100)
insertText:
{n}By all Plaintiffs against all Defendants, Plaintiffs reallege, and incorporate by reference every allegation contained in the preceding paragraphs of this Complaint as though set forth herein.
{n}The California Elder Abuse and Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act, codified in California Welfare and Institutions Code § 15600, et seq., seeks to prevent the abuse, neglect, or abandonment of elders and dependent adults who are more vulnerable and at risk of mistreatment.
{n}Plaintiff Darlene Brown is of a class of people this statute was designed and intended to protect. California Welfare and Institutions Code § 15610.27 defines an elder as "any person residing in this state, 65 years of age or older." Plaintiff meets this definition because Plaintiff Darlene Brown is 65 years of age.
{n}Abuse of an elder or a dependent adult is defined by California Welfare and Institutions Code § 15610.07 as "(1) Physical abuse, neglect, abandonment, isolation, abduction, or other treatment with resulting physical harm or pain or mental suffering."
{n}"Neglect" is specified to mean "(1) The negligent failure of any person having the care or custody of an elder or a dependent adult to exercise that degree of care that a reasonably person in a like position would exercise" pursuant to California Welfare and Institutions Code § 15610.57(a). Further, "neglect includes, but is not limited to ... (3) Failure to protect from health and safety hazards." (California Welfare and Institutions Code § 15610.57(6).)
{n}While Defendants are not the direct custodians of the elder Plaintiff Darlene Brown, the Defendants, as landlords to the tenant Plaintiffs, have a duty to exercise care as owners, managers, and operators of the Subject Property in which the elder Plaintiff Darlene Brown resides.
{n}The Defendants, by and through their gross abandonment and neglect in the maintenance and repairs of the Subject Property, failed to exercise a degree of care that a reasonable person in a like position would exercise, and through this negligence failed to protect the elder Plaintiff Darlene Brown from health and safety hazards.
{n}The Defendants instead forced the elder Plaintiff to live in conditions that resulted or reasonably would result in physical harm, pain, and mental suffering.
{n}The elder Plaintiff Darlene Brown was and is in no condition to protect or defend themselves from this mistreatment, and Defendants took advantage of their vulnerability, choosing to ignore complaints and refuse to provide repairs, forcing the vulnerable Plaintiff Darlene Brown to suffer in neglected, abysmal conditions.
{n}By and through this conduct, Defendants knew, or reasonably should have known that they were engaging in and contributing to the abuse of the elder Plaintiff Darlene Brown, and thus were in violation of the California Elder Abuse and Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act.
{n}There is clear and convincing evidence that Defendants, as the landlords of the Property in which the elder Plaintiff resided, engaged in negligent behavior, as defined under California Welfare and Institutions Code § 15610.57.
{n}Defendants systematic behavior resulting in the aforementioned injuries to Plaintiffs was and is deliberate and undertaken with malice, oppression, and recklessness. Thus, pursuant to California Welfare and Institutions Code § 15657, Plaintiffs are entitled to their reasonable attorneys' fees and costs and punitive damages.
{n}Plaintiffs further pray under California Welfare and Institutions Code § 15657.03(b)(1) for an order enjoining the Defendants from engaging in the harassment, intimidation, and retaliation against Plaintiff.

heading:
Intentional Influence to Vacate
(Against All Defendants and DOES 1-100)
insertText:
{n}By all Plaintiffs against all Defendants, Plaintiffs incorporate herein by reference every allegation contained in the preceding paragraphs of this Complaint as though set forth herein.
{n}California Civil Code § 1940.2 provides in pertinent part: (a) It is unlawful for a landlord to do any of the following for the purpose of influencing a tenant to vacate a dwelling: (1) Engage in conduct that violates subdivision (a) of Section 484 of the Penal Code. (2) Engage in conduct that violates Section 518 of the Penal Code. (3) Use, or threaten to use, force, willful threats, or menacing conduct constituting a course of conduct that interferes with the tenant's quiet enjoyment of the premises in violation of Section 1927 that would create an apprehension of harm in a reasonable person. Nothing in this paragraph requires a tenant to be actually or constructively evicted in order to obtain relief. (4) Commit a significant and intentional violation of Section 1954.
{n}Defendants were required to provide Plaintiffs with a quiet enjoyment of the premises.
{n}Civil Code § 1940.2(a)(3) prohibits landlords from using or threatening to use force, making willful threats, or behaving menacingly in a way that interferes with a tenant's quiet enjoyment of the premises and that would create an apprehension of harm in a reasonable person. Defendants' retaliatory conduct is in contravention of Civil Code § 1940.2(a)(3).
{n}Defendants used force, willful threats, and menacing conduct constituting a course of conduct that interferes with the Plaintiffs' quiet enjoyment of the premises in violation of Section 1927 and creating an apprehension of harm in a reasonable person. Specifically, Plaintiffs are informed and believe and thereon allege Defendants engaged in a pattern and practice of harassing the Plaintiffs with the intention of influencing the Plaintiffs to vacate the Property.
{n}Plaintiffs allege the Defendants have harassed the Plaintiffs by giving the Plaintiffs an illegal, oppressive rent increase of 33.33%, from $750 per month to $1,000 per month, refusing to accept the Plaintiffs' rent when they refused to pay the new, illegal rent amount, trespassing in the Subject Property, verbally harassing the Plaintiffs, serving the Plaintiffs with harassing notices of termination of tenancy and notices to quit, and making threatening comments about eviction and forcing Plaintiff out of the home, with the intention and effect of interfering with the Plaintiffs' quiet enjoyment of the premises, and leaving the Plaintiff afraid of eviction and retaliation, in violation of Civil Code § 1940.2(a)(3). Plaintiffs allege the Defendants are engaging in this behavior with the intention of influencing the Plaintiffs to vacate the Property.
{n}As a direct result of the conduct of Defendant, Plaintiffs have suffered general and special damages to be determined at the time of trial.
{n}It is alleged that all Defendants, by and through their respective managing agents, acted intentionally and maliciously for the purpose of obtaining the vacancy of the Plaintiffs from the Subject Property and in retaliation for having made complaints to government agencies and/or legal counsel. Said conduct was in direct violation of Section 1940.2 of the Civil Code. Plaintiffs pray for the statutory remedies as provided in Section 1940.2 of the Civil Code as well as other remedies for damages.
{n}The conduct of Defendants, as alleged hereinabove, was malicious, oppressive, and despicable in that said conduct was ongoing and was designed to force Plaintiffs to give up their rent-stabilized possession of the Subject Property at substantial economic harm to Plaintiffs herein, for the sole economic benefit of Defendant, and was done in retaliation for Plaintiffs asserting their legal rights. As a result of said conduct, Plaintiffs are entitled to punitive and exemplary damages.

heading:
Toxic Environmental Mold Tort
(Against All Defendants and DOES 1-100)
insertText:
{n}By all Plaintiffs against all Defendants, Plaintiffs reallege and incorporate by reference every allegation contained in the preceding paragraphs of this Complaint as though set forth herein.
{n}On information and belief, the Plaintiffs were exposed to the toxic mold in the Subject Property for an extended period of time, including in or around three years.
{n}The Subject Property suffers from extensive mold contamination in the bathroom and kitchen, as a direct and proximate result of plumbing leaks in the Property. Plaintiffs allege there is mold growth on the bathtub and sinks, and beneath the sinks where frequent leaking has caused the wood of the cabinets to warp, bow, and deteriorate. The Plaintiffs allege that after years of complaints, the Defendants have utterly failed to address the mold contamination. Plaintiffs allege they then contacted the Los Angeles Housing Department, who cited the Defendants for the mold growth on or around April 25, 2023. Plaintiffs allege that only after citation did the Defendants begin remediating the mold growth.
{n}Plaintiffs are informed and believe and thereon allege that mold particles, especially toxic mold, can trigger and/or cause asthma symptoms and respiratory illness symptoms, including shortness of breath, wheezing or coughing, and lung irritation. Plaintiffs are informed and believe and thereon allege that mold particles, especially toxic mold, can trigger or cause allergies, including allergy symptoms such as sneezing, nose, mouth, or throat irritation, irritated eyes, runny nose, and other various symptoms.
{n}The Plaintiffs, as a result of their exposure to the mold, have developed new and/or worsened illnesses. The Plaintiff Dakora Robinson has a young child who has asthma, and when {pronoun_possessive} child spends more than a few hours inside the Property, {pronoun_possessive} asthma worsens in intensity and frequency. The Plaintiffs both suffer from constant, oppressive cold and flu like symptoms, headaches, fatigue, and other related symptoms. Plaintiff Darlene Brown is 65 years old, and alleges {pronoun_possessive} age combined with the mold exposure leave {pronoun_possessive} at risk for serious illness. The Plaintiffs have both had to miss work and suffered decreases to their quality of life as a direct and proximate result of their exposure to the mold growth in the Property.
{n}As a result of their exposure, toxins from the toxic mold entered the Plaintiffs' body by and through their respiratory airways, where the particulate matter entered the Plaintiffs' lungs and body, and caused them to experience respiratory illness and injury.
{n}Plaintiffs are informed and believe and thereon allege that their exposure to the toxic mold was the direct and proximate result of their physical injury, as the symptoms they experienced did not, on information and belief, exist to this extent prior to the exposure to mold in the Property. Plaintiffs allege that these symptoms lessen when they are away from the Property.
{n}The Plaintiffs now suffer from physical ailments and injury, and they allege that their exposure to toxic mold which entered their bodies by and through their breathing in mold for in or around three years was a substantial factor in bringing about, prolonging, and/or aggravating their illness.
{n}The Defendants knew there was dangerous mold contamination in the Property, were placed on notice of the presence of the mold and its impacts on the Plaintiffs' health by and through the Plaintiffs' complaints, and still, the Defendants did nothing to suitably, sufficiently address the issue.
{n}As a direct and proximate result of Defendant's conduct, Plaintiffs have suffered and/or continue to suffer illness, physical injury, property damages, emotional harm, anguish, depression, fearfulness, anxiety, nightmares, difficulty sleeping, embarrassment, shame, and other economic damages in an amount to be determined according to proof at trial.
{n}Defendants' conduct was deliberate and undertaken with oppression, fraud, and/or malice within the meaning provided in Civil Code section 3294, justifying an award of punitive damages sufficient to punish Defendants and to deter them from such misconduct in the future.

checkboxText: Failure to Abate Unsafe Mold Conditions in Violation of California Habitability Laws
heading:
FAILURE TO ABATE UNSAFE MOLD CONDITIONS IN VIOLATION OF CALIFORNIA HABITABILITY LAWS
(Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1941, 1941.1, 1942.4; Health & Safety Code §§ 17920.3, 26147; Los Angeles Housing Code § 91.8903)
(By PLAINTIFF Against All DEFENDANTS and DOES 1–25)
insertText:
{n}PLAINTIFF realleges and incorporates by reference all preceding paragraphs of this Complaint as though fully set forth herein.
{n}DEFENDANTS, and each of them, were at all relevant times the owners, landlords, property managers, or agents responsible for the maintenance and habitability of the PROPERTY rented to PLAINTIFF.
{n}During PLAINTIFF’s tenancy, the PROPERTY became uninhabitable due to the presence of mold and moisture intrusion, caused by water leaks, plumbing defects, poor ventilation, and/or a failure to repair water damage in a timely and adequate manner.
{n}Pursuant to California Civil Code §§ 1941 and 1941.1, rental units must be maintained in a condition fit for human occupation, including being free from dampness, mold, and conditions that endanger the health of occupants. Additionally, Health & Safety Code § 17920.3 explicitly includes visible mold and water intrusion as conditions that render a dwelling “substandard.”
{n}Under California Civil Code § 1942.4, landlords are prohibited from collecting rent when a property violates habitability standards and the landlord has been notified of the condition but fails to remediate it within a reasonable time.
{n}Local regulations, including Los Angeles Municipal Code § 91.8903, empower building officials to declare a property substandard where mold growth exists due to long-standing water damage, which DEFENDANTS failed to correct despite having received multiple notices and complaints from PLAINTIFF.
{n}DEFENDANTS’ failure to investigate, properly remediate, or abate the mold conditions demonstrates negligence, disregard for tenant safety, and a violation of their statutory duty to provide a habitable living environment.
{n}As a direct and proximate result of DEFENDANTS’ conduct and omissions, PLAINTIFF suffered and continues to suffer adverse health effects including respiratory distress, sinus infections, fatigue, and anxiety as well as emotional distress, loss of use and enjoyment of the premises, potential medical costs, and property damage.
{n}PLAINTIFF seeks damages in an amount to be proven at trial, including but not limited to: recovery of rent paid during the period of uninhabitability, compensation for personal injuries and property damage, statutory penalties, attorneys’ fees and costs, and such other relief as the Court may deem just and proper.
